item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much of this was too TOO technical and a bit confusing. I'm guessing the "deformed" people mentioned in the unclassified version were people not affected by the parasite…and I like the final most heavily classified part; but all the neuroscience is a bit too heavy science for most readers and I'm giving it a -1 for that reason.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I've been meaning to take out the mention of antimemetics in the skip, as I feel I used it as more of a copout here than anything.

Totally cool with the other 3 links (3334, 711, and Iteration 0). Though if you can find any other machines/skips similar in function to the EXPERTISE, that'd also be a good place to link something, I feel.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Firstly, this mentions that the Foundation is developing a "counter meme" to SCP-3031 with "intensive study." I propose crosslinking to SCP-3334 here, because the point of SCP-3334 is creating memetic things with extensive testing.

Secondly, this mentions a "Extra-Temporal/Reality Receiver" that receives data from the future. This sounds similar to SCP-711, which is a device that receives information from the future. I propose linking to that.

Thirdly, "antimemetic effects" are brought up in this skip. I propose linking to We Need to Talk About Fifty-Five, which introduces the antimemetic concept quite nicely.

Finally, this skip brings up a device apparently created by Dr. Thaddeus Xyank, who has a tale about him. I propose linking to that.

Editing this:

Intensive study has developed a countermemetic agent capable of suppressing and neutralizing SCP-3031-2's effects

To this:

Intensive study has developed a countermemetic agent capable of suppressing and neutralizing SCP-3031-2's effects

Editing this:

All documentation and information regarding SCP-3031 has been pieced together from data fragments received from the Extra-Temporal/Reality Receiver located in Communications Site-119.1

To this:

All documentation and information regarding SCP-3031 has been pieced together from data fragments received from the Extra-Temporal/Reality Receiver located in Communications Site-119.2

Editing this:

SCP-3031 possesses a mild antimemetic effect preventing it from being distinguished from similar glial cells, which has made detailed study difficult.

To this:

SCP-3031 possesses a mild antimemetic3 effect preventing it from being distinguished from similar glial cells, which has made detailed study difficult.

Editing this:

Construct an Enhanced Xyank-Palanez Real-Temporal Shift Equalizer (EXPERTISE) capable of safely tunnelling at least 106 years in time.

To this:

Construct an Enhanced Xyank-Palanez Real-Temporal Shift Equalizer (EXPERTISE) capable of safely tunnelling at least 106 years in time.

Footnotes
1. The ETRR is designed to receive and intercept temporally- and reality-shifted transmissions.
2. The ETRR is designed to receive and intercept temporally- and reality-shifted transmissions. The ETRR is based off of designs reverse-engineered from those used to construct SCP-711.
3. For more information on antimemes, please consult Document 055-Sigma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The scariest part about this is how much it explains. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if we are the fearless humans and that's why we can't perceive anomalies the way the people in the scp foundation can? 2sppooky

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's way too bogged down in its own technicalities to provide a worthwhile glimpse into the actually interesting parts. I'd have loved to see more wordspace dedicated to building up and ramping up this huge threat we're supposed to prepare for, or some sweeping tale of civilisation-wide hubris. Instead I get a note at the end explaining it all in trite metaphors. Where's the fun?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of this makes me want to read a tale about how these fearless humans lived. Are they witless, idiot heroes like in a lot of fiction or are they ruthless, calculating sociopaths like the others in this discussion have guessed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember, 3031 feeds on neuron signals, then sends its own out instead. (Immunity to it causes problems because it's so widespread, humanity basically evolved around it).

The Foundation still doesn't have complete knowledge of 3031, so for now they'll classify it as a parasite for feeding on thoughts; even though it's not as harmful as say, if it ate the neurons themselves (probably). Also, any problems that could stem from elevated fear (irrational phobias, certain anxiety disorders, etc.) are pretty much 3031's fault now. I'd say that's a detriment.

But who knows? Maybe in the future they'll reclassify it when they uncover more about it. After all, it only appears to be a parasite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3031 appears to be an engineered parasite

How can the organism be a parasite when it isn't conferring a net harm on its host? A parasite decreases the overall fitness of its host; this seems to be INCREASING the overall fitness of humans. In that case, it would be a mutualistic relationship, not a parasitic one.

item.title: 21st place :P
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, what a ride. What a contest!

Thanks to all, I'm proud to share this moment of history with you :)

And everyone who said this wasn't scary enough for 3000, well, the contest is over and it's 3031 now, so get back here! I've made a few small edits, and will mess with this a bit more tomorrow.

Here's to Series 4!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fear is the foundation of courage

You know at first I was afraid that part was too blatant, then I was afraid it was too subtle. Either way, both fears have been put to rest thanks to you, the first person to mention it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly just realized that… Indirectly… Scp-3000 is Special Containment Procedures themselves. Since it is what gave us the third option. Not to run, or fight, but TO CONTAIN.
Scp-3000 started the SCP. Along with probably 5 other SCP's in cooperation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just now realizing that I may have subconsciously and accidentally borrowed pieces of 1000, Moose :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fair enough!

I'm not typically a fan of technobabble or hard sci-fi on this site, but the idea of swiss cheese time machines was too good to not mention in my post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your thoughts!

Nah, I actually was secretly hoping for at least one person to think the swiss cheese time machine was cool. All I needed was fun technobabble to deliver the SCP, so I had a little guilty fun when making that part.

I get what you're saying about the tropes and devices I used. Petrograd, Moose, and Conwell already mentioned that it's kinda derivative. I'm not as innovative as I'd like to be, but I guess I was hoping that the concept would be strong enough to get by on its own merits with a rather standard presentation. The caramel center of this work is the concept. Everything else, the specific biology of the parasite, the cover story, the note, all of that is just window dressing, and if I ever figure out a better way to present it then maybe I'll tear the whole thing down and do it over.

It's not really me trying to be derivative or trying to manipulate people's reactions. I just recognize that these devices are used because they work, and even if they're kind of overplayed at this point, I'd hoped that they'd be good for one more use.

Also, when I write, a key factor is "would I enjoy it if someone else wrote it?" In my case, I love those classic tropes and devices, but I recognize and accept that not everyone else will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much enjoy how you made the concept of the contest into the skip itself (so to speak), but the article almost never grabs me otherwise (the only other time it ever interested me was the talk about sending an anomaly back in time through time tunnels, which wasn't what you're aiming for I gather). I'm also super tired by the fact that I knew that the moment I clicked the first collapsible, it'd be The Same SCP But Described Differently. I think this is mostly my taste, but I also think it's a bit too reliant on site tropes that have been proven to work and get reactions out of people. I could see myself liking this concept, but not as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly don't know how I feel about this. The concept is very clever, and the rest is very straightforward to support it. But that makes me a little less interested (even if it's got also-clever references to some basic Foundation tropes I really like… and some of my own work!).

The cleverness might be enough to make it work for me. Have to think about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, yes. This. Very this.

Remember, kids, the only thing we have to fear, is the floating disembodied head of Winston Churchill! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly the addition of that idea would make for a much more interesting narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm, you're right. I'll probably add that in after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So since the introduction of the parasite 100,000 years ago, humans have evolved to become dependant on it? If so, you might want to say that explicitly, it'd make things clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did as much research as I could to ensure minimal handwaving and maximum believability, and I did learn that the amygdala is responsible for more than just fear.

Emotional awareness in general, which is why sociopaths often have underdeveloped amygdala, and why tumors or amygdala damage often results in psychotic behavior.

A society of sociopaths, undone by an ontoclysm, trying to stop a cosmic horror that can be manipulated by fear…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that I'm looking forward to seeing in the future though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it has much to do with 'scariness'. I believe the concern is that it's not introduced anywhere in the narrative or foreshadowed, so it seems tacked into the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fair. I assume you've read my detailed reply to psul below too. After the contest I'll have to decide whether to expand on the boogeyman's importance, or to leave it be since it won't need to be scary after the 20th.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extremely well written, great twist, and I think it fits the theme extremely well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was about to make a comment identical to this. I was then going to expand a bit so my post wasn't just "ye i agree", but then I scrolled down and saw psul had explained perfectly why I didn't find it scary. In particular, let me shine a spotlight on their line saying that "this boogeyman doesn't cast a shadow on the text".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, while I find the whole "The actual SCP document is in another castle" shtick to be a little overdone, the twist that was contained within the second document was interesting enough to hold my attention. Additionally, I do like the desperation present in the final note, even if the content therein gets a little narmy at times.

+1. Neat article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little of both, but more important is the fact that if 3000 isn't 'welcome', so to speak, that's a lot of important connections and myelin formation the brain is missing out on.

I'll use the metaphor of a car driving. For those completely immune, 3000 can't get in at all and the car is missing 3 tires. For those resistant, one tire is spinning normally, and two are spinning backwards and one might be misaligned and wearing on the axle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to SCP-3000's tendency to irreversibly integrate itself into neural systems, immunity to the parasite severely inhibits the rate of formation of myelin sheaths in the neurons of the brain, leading to complications.

That's rather confusing. For those who are immune/resistant, does 3000 force its way into their brains, but fails to properly synchronize with the surrounding brain cells, so its effects on surrounding brain cells is random and hence deleterious? Are their brain cells constantly fighting off 3000, and the effort of fighting it off prevents them from working properly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did as much research as I could to ensure minimal handwaving and maximum believability, and I did learn that the amygdala is responsible for more than just fear. Memory too, but "Anantashesha" and others have covered that angle far better than I could.

I was originally planning an interview with a 3000-1 victim, but I barely finished this thing in time as it is, and my interview writing skills are not up to par yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of interest is that I remember the amygdala is responsible for other interesting aspects of sociability and intelligence. I'm not sure if you're allowed to edit it in now (or intended for the relation), but alterations to the amygdala would likely have fundamental impacts on how humanity would behave as as a social species.

If in our present reality our 'normal' amygdala is actually relatively 'enlarged', then a brief purview of individuals with damaged or nonfunctional amygdala would give a better glimpse into the past (past's future?) reality before infection occurred.

I enjoyed this one because it didn't get too hand-wavy with the science, and was actually interesting in the implications.

edit: fixed some missing words

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not quite.

Spoilers, I guess
– hide block

The original future doesn't have a Foundation. Note that different terminology is used, most significantly that Esperanza isn't an O5.

That reality was more complacent, lacking in fear. They sent fear-amplifying parasites back in time to start our timeline, hoping that fear of the unknown would jumpstart our defense against anomalies, create things like the Foundation, and keep the world-ender at bay.

3000 itself is still anomalous and not completely understood, so I figured it would warrant SCP classification even if virtually everyone is infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I don't get it. I don't think.

+ Is this right?
– hide block

A Foundation from the future in another dimension sent fear-dulling parasites back in time to integrate them with humanity's nervous system and allow us to fight back against the 'gods' of old so there won't be as many anomalies today? I feel stupid even asking this.

Also, if over 99% of the population is infected with this, wouldn't that make the 60,000-some people who aren't the actual anomaly? I mean, it sounds like we need it to live and function properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. As I slowly develop my site writing style, I'm realizing that my work here is usually derived from or inspired by existing work.

When I conceived this, I knew it was going to be ambitious and tricky to execute. So it's nice to hear comments like this, especially from experienced writers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. Simple, for what it is, and reliant on a lot of old tropes (escalating clearance, note at the end), but all quite well executed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there anything of which it may be said, “See, this is new”? It has already been in ancient times before us.
-Ecclesiastes 1:10

I seem to have a recurring thing where things I write have already been done similarly. I don't think I ever got the chance to read yours, but I hope you like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strange, I posted something very similar to this on the mainlist two or three years ago. Not accusing you of anything of course, because they're different enough. You did it way better than I did anyway, evidenced by the fact that my article is gone now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the world-ender: I don't think my writing skills are developed enough to write the next 2317. I knew if I focused on the entity itself, it would likely fall flat as "yet another boring all-devouring entity", which we already have. The "affected by our perception" bit is me trying to customize it, as well as tie it into the narrative.

This was related to my fridge logic conclusion that this was the Daeva history. Fear Alone suggests this very mechanism for appeasing the Scarlet King/Devourer. djoric has suggested that the Daeva deliberately triggered their own ontoclysm in order to stop the manifestation of the Scarlet King. I think it would have been a great twist for the Daevite history in the present of their worldline to use time travel to trigger the ontoclysm. This also explains why SCP-140-A (and his predecessors) are trying to use the Chronicles to undo it, because they are from the past and see the ontoclysm as an attack from the future rather than a choice that needed to be made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will address your points individually.

  • On the convenience of the time travel: I knew that would be a sticking point for some when I started this, as the acclaimed Tim Duncan skip received the same criticism. For all we know, Reality 1 already had an EXPERTISE collecting dust, with no real reason to use it. They are several centuries ahead, after all.
  • On the precise targeting of 3000: I did a lot of research into neurology when writing this, but I can only fudge so much science. I'm gonna have to leave that as plot convenience, though I tried my best to at least make it somewhat plausible.
  • On the world-ender: I don't think my writing skills are developed enough to write the next 2317. I knew if I focused on the entity itself, it would likely fall flat as "yet another boring all-devouring entity", which we already have. The "affected by our perception" bit is me trying to customize it, as well as tie it into the narrative. It's not described much, as I was attempting to use the indirect horror as seen in Kalinin's 2798.
  • On the note: Yes, I'm very aware that final notes are a controversial plot device. I am not used to writing prose like this. I started out by repurposing the Note from the Administrator, and by the end had added several other allegories and threads. Esperanza is meant to sound somewhat flowery, but that's fair if it's not your taste. If I had had more time, I might have tried overhauling the note to arrange the threads better, because I can see your point that it's kind of Frankenstein'd together. And out of universe, I was trying to find a killer finishing metaphor.
  • On the explanatory power of the note: Esperanza has now realized that even if the rewritten reality can beat the entity, there will be so much more they have to stand up to. Therefore, best to describe the general mission, and the weakness of the entity. I don't feel that "by the way, it's a mile-tall smoke monster with tentacles for eyes" would help as much.

Thank you for your thoughts, though; I'll certainly take them into account if I polish this up after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see the thematic point, but I'm afraid this wasn't scary of itself - not least because if the Foundation has science sufficiently worked out to allow time-travel for the infection of early humans with an engineered brain parasite that won't affect cognition other than activating fear (and the unintended consequence risks of that are immense), then to be honest I don't think they need to be scared of anything. Particularly when this boogeyman doesn't even cast a shadow on the text.

It's all a little too pat and convenient for me, and the final note (again, the final note trope) keeps circling around the same points like it's trying to find a killer finishing metaphor, rather than just explaining what's going on in a clearly understandable way. I'm afraid my suspension of belief was destroyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yeah, you totally should've posted it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fair, and I don't want you to think I ignored you by posting anyway. I just wanted to get this out there and participate in the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said about the draft, I think this is a great meta-horror, but isn't actually scary. I will withhold my vote for ten days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knowing you've read so many of the other great entries in this contest, it really means a lot to me to hear you say that. I'm touched, I really am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope it's not too late for this to win. This struck me in a way none of the other contest entries have had. Its not the scariest, I think; but it is, thematically, the most appropriate. I would love to see this get the spot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the upvote!

About "passed off", you might be right, but it's probably too late to change that :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm…alright, you know what? I personally didn't find it scary, but it's interesting enough that I'm willing to upvote. One quibble though- the phrase "passed off" in the Containment Procedures seems to casual for an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No no, fear definitely did exist in the previous reality ("the fear of the survivors…"). It just wasn't expressed as strongly, nor was it as tightly bound to the human psyche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not 100% sure I get this. Are you saying that fear itself was created by some guys in a lab to provide a defense against some entity? If so, how would the people in the future even know what fear was, let alone how to create it? It might just be because I' m tired right now, but this doesn't make much sense to me.

item.title: JUST DO IT!
item.content:encoded:
As with everything I do… waited till the last minute. Oh well. Deadlines are a great motivator.

Special thanks to Soulless and DrBleep for giving me the motivation and self-confidence to actually do this, Zyn and sirpudding on the forums, and everyone who took a look in chat (Oboe, Bluemorpho, and Westrin, I think).

Very special thanks to The Administrator, whose classic note I repurposed/remixed/reinterpreted/reimagined.

Photo is public domain, sourced from here.

And since sirpudding brought it up, no, this has nothing to do with the Daevites.

The Contest begins, my friends.
